original_abstract: Controlling the surface strain—the compression or expansion of surface atoms—is a powerful strategy for tuning the chemistry and catalytic efficiency of nanoparticles (NPs). In this work, we demonstrate how the surface platinum (Pt) strain in core/shell FePt/Pt NPs, featuring a three-atomic-layer Pt shell, can be precisely modulated by inducing a structural transition in the core from a cubic solid solution (fcc) to a tetragonal intermetallic (fct) framework. Our findings show that fct-FePt/Pt NPs exhibit significantly enhanced activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) simulations suggest that this performance boost stems from the fct core’s ability to relieve the overcompressed strain within the Pt shell. We further optimized this effect by partially substituting Fe with Cu to form fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs. These trimetallic catalysts emerged as the most efficient for ORR, delivering a specific activity nearly 10 times higher than that of luxury-grade commercial Pt catalysts. This structure-driven control over surface strain provides a robust new blueprint for designing and optimizing nanoparticle catalysts for ORR and a wide range of other chemical applications.
humanized_abstract: Engineered surface strain is a commonly used variable to modify the surface chemistry and catalytic performances of nanoparticles (NPs). Here, we show that the surface strain of the outer platinum (Pt) shell of a core/shell FePt/Pt NP, which consists of a 3 Pt layer thick shell on top of a fcc solid solution core of an intermetallic fct FePt alloy, can be systematically adjusted by transitioning the FePt alloy core from the fcc state to the fct state. It is demonstrated that fct-FePt/Pt NPs have greater catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The improved catalytic performance of the fct-FePt/Pt NPs was elucidated using the hybrid quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) method, which indicated that the fct core alleviated the overcompressed state of the Pt shell. In addition, by partially replacing Fe with Cu to form fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs, the catalytic performances of the NPs were further enhanced, exhibiting the highest specific ORR activity that is approximately 10 times higher than that of a luxury grade Pt catalyst. This work thus provides a new way to systematically regulate the surface strain of NPs to improve their catalytic activities.

It is demonstrated that fct-FePt/Pt NPs have greater catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The improved catalytic performance of the fct-FePt/Pt NPs was elucidated using the hybrid quantum mechanics–molecular mechanics (QM–MM) method, which indicated that the fct core alleviated the overcompressed state of the Pt shell. In addition, by partially replacing Fe with Cu to form fct-FeCuPt/Pt NPs, the catalytic performances of the NPs were further enhanced, exhibiting the highest specific ORR activity that is approximately 10 times higher than that of a luxury grade Pt catalyst.
